{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52440","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52440/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52440/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52440/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52440","id":2030767017,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM55Cwup","number":52440,"title":"Can we speed up objectid using faster hashing","user":{"login":"Zentrik","id":19725290,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzI1Mjkw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19725290?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik","html_url":"https://github.com/Zentrik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-12-07T13:29:14Z","updated_at":"2023-12-07T13:29:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I have an `IdDict` with `SimpleVector` keys, I see a lot of time is spent calling `jl_object_id_` to hash the keys, specifically [int64hash](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/856e1120a8f255e4907a05f0146a2026a6665dd4/src/support/hashing.c#L28), which is called to hash any 64 bit elements in the key and it's also called in [bitmix](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/856e1120a8f255e4907a05f0146a2026a6665dd4/src/support/hashing.h#L29). 

It seems `jl_object_id_` is mainly used for hashtables like `IdDict` and `IdSet` in which case would it be fine to switch to something like [FxHasher](https://docs.rs/rustc-hash/latest/rustc_hash/) (used in the rust compiler) to replace both `bitmix` and `int64hash`?
Replacing `bitmix` in `hash_svec` with FxHasher gives a 10-100x improvement on `@benchmark hash(x) setup=x=Core.svec(rand(Int64, 10^n)...)` for n in 1 to 6.

However, FxHasher for 64 bit ints is just a multiplication by a constant, though if objectid is only used in internal hashtables, like `IdDict`, I think this should be fine. Whilst objectid is used to hash general objects on the Julia side, its passed through a hash so I don't think it should affect `hash` in Julia too much.

Alternatively, we could try using the non AES intrinsic version of ahash, this should still be much faster than what we have currently and higher quality than FxHasher. Again, ahash says it's specifically for hashtables.

Alternatively, to replace only `int64hash`, [Squirrel3](https://gist.github.com/Zentrik/b5053668935102719ebf619944ce3e6a) is quite simple and seems to be reasonably fast (about 1.5-2x faster than int64hash) and should have higher quality hashes than FxHasher.

Does it seem reasonable to make a change along these lines? I see that currently `int64hash` is [invertible](https://gist.github.com/lh3/974ced188be2f90422cc), do we care about this? I don't see it being used anywhere.

This would also speed up my robinhood implementation of `IdDict`.


","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52440/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52440/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

hashing","user":{"login":"Zentrik","id":19725290,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzI1Mjkw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19725290?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik","html_url":"https://github.com/Zentrik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-12-07T13:29:14Z","updated_at":"2023-12-11T18:30:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I have an `IdDict` with `SimpleVector` keys, I see a lot of time is spent calling `jl_object_id_` to hash the keys, specifically [int64hash](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/856e1120a8f255e4907a05f0146a2026a6665dd4/src/support/hashing.c#L28), which is called to hash any 64 bit elements in the key and it's also called in [bitmix](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/856e1120a8f255e4907a05f0146a2026a6665dd4/src/support/hashing.h#L29). 

It seems `jl_object_id_` is mainly used for hashtables like `IdDict` and `IdSet` in which case would it be fine to switch to something like [FxHasher](https://docs.rs/rustc-hash/latest/rustc_hash/) (used in the rust compiler) to replace both `bitmix` and `int64hash`?
Replacing `bitmix` in `hash_svec` with FxHasher gives a 10-100x improvement on `@benchmark hash(x) setup=x=Core.svec(rand(Int64, 10^n)...)` for n in 1 to 6.

However, FxHasher for 64 bit ints is just a multiplication by a constant, though if objectid is only used in internal hashtables, like `IdDict`, I think this should be fine. Whilst objectid is used to hash general objects on the Julia side, its passed through a hash so I don't think it should affect `hash` in Julia too much.

Alternatively, we could try using the non AES intrinsic version of ahash, this should still be much faster than what we have currently and higher quality than FxHasher. Again, ahash says it's specifically for hashtables.

Alternatively, to replace only `int64hash`, [Squirrel3](https://gist.github.com/Zentrik/b5053668935102719ebf619944ce3e6a) is quite simple and seems to be reasonably fast (about 1.5-2x faster than int64hash) and should have higher quality hashes than FxHasher.

Does it seem reasonable to make a change along these lines? I see that currently `int64hash` is [invertible](https://gist.github.com/lh3/974ced188be2f90422cc), do we care about this? I don't see it being used anywhere.

This would also speed up my robinhood implementation of `IdDict`.

we speed up objectid using faster hashing","user":{"login":"Zentrik","id":19725290,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzI1Mjkw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19725290?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik","html_url":"https://github.com/Zentrik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115858,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU4NTg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/performance","name":"performance","color":"d7e102","default":false,"description":"Must go faster"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":10,"created_at":"2023-12-07T13:29:14Z","updated_at":"2023-12-12T22:23:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I have an `IdDict` with `SimpleVector` keys, I see a lot of time is spent calling `jl_object_id_` to hash the keys, specifically [int64hash](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/856e1120a8f255e4907a05f0146a2026a6665dd4/src/support/hashing.c#L28), which is called to hash any 64 bit elements in the key and it's also called in [bitmix](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/856e1120a8f255e4907a05f0146a2026a6665dd4/src/support/hashing.h#L29). 

It seems `jl_object_id_` is mainly used for hashtables like `IdDict` and `IdSet` in which case would it be fine to switch to something like [FxHasher](https://docs.rs/rustc-hash/latest/rustc_hash/) (used in the rust compiler) to replace both `bitmix` and `int64hash`?
Replacing `bitmix` in `hash_svec` with FxHasher gives a 10-100x improvement on `@benchmark hash(x) setup=x=Core.svec(rand(Int64, 10^n)...)` for n in 1 to 6.

However, FxHasher for 64 bit ints is just a multiplication by a constant, though if objectid is only used in internal hashtables, like `IdDict`, I think this should be fine. Whilst objectid is used to hash general objects on the Julia side, its passed through a hash so I don't think it should affect `hash` in Julia too much.

Alternatively, we could try using the non AES intrinsic version of ahash, this should still be much faster than what we have currently and higher quality than FxHasher. Again, ahash says it's specifically for hashtables.

Alternatively, to replace only `int64hash`, [Squirrel3](https://gist.github.com/Zentrik/b5053668935102719ebf619944ce3e6a) is quite simple and seems to be reasonably fast (about 1.5-2x faster than int64hash) and should have higher quality hashes than FxHasher.

Does it seem reasonable to make a change along these lines? I see that currently `int64hash` is [invertible](https://gist.github.com/lh3/974ced188be2f90422cc), do we care about this? I don't see it being used anywhere.

This would also speed up my robinhood implementation of `IdDict`.

order"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-11-03T20:27:11Z","updated_at":"2023-11-05T21:02:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This is the recommended way to sort by rows with arrays according to the docs.  There really needs to be a way to do this that is type stable.  This is an old issue but was never resolved.

```
function main()
    array_to_sort = rand(2,3)
    sorted = sortslices(array_to_sort; dims=1)
end

@code_warntype main() 
 ```

```
MethodInstance for main()
  from main() @ Main Untitled-1:2
Arguments
  #self#::Core.Const(main)
Locals
  sorted::Any
  array_to_sort::Matrix{Float64}
Body::Any
1 ─      (array_to_sort = Main.rand(2, 3))
│   %2 = (:dims,)::Core.Const((:dims,))
│   %3 = Core.apply_type(Core.NamedTuple, %2)::Core.Const(NamedTuple{(:dims,)})
│   %4 = Core.tuple(1)::Core.Const((1,))
│   %5 = (%3)(%4)::Core.Const((dims = 1,))
│   %6 = Core.kwcall(%5, Main.sortslices, array_to_sort)::Any
│        (sorted = %6)
└──      return %6
```
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52019/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52019/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1793844909","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52019#issuecomment-1793844909","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52019","id":1793844909,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5q6-at","user":{"login":"Zentrik","id":19725290,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzI1Mjkw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19725290?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik","html_url":"https://github.com/Zentrik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Zentrik/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-11-05T21:02:46Z","updated_at":"2023-11-05T21:02:46Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The problem seems to be that we're calling this which tries to convert the `dims` argument to a `Val`
```jl
function sortslices(A::AbstractArray; dims::Union{Integer, Tuple{Vararg{Integer}}}, kws...)
    _sortslices(A, Val{dims}(); kws...)
end
```
This causes `@code_warntype` and Cthulhu to the type of `Val{dims}` is unknown which causes all the type instabilities.
E.g. 
```jl
function main_val()
    array_to_sort = rand(2,3)
    sorted = Base._sortslices(array_to_sort, Val{1}())
end

@code_warntype main_val() 
```
```jl
julia> @code_warntype main_val() 
MethodInstance for main_val()
  from main_val() @ Main REPL[32]:1
Arguments
  #self#::Core.Const(main_val)
Locals
  sorted::Matrix{Float64}
  array_to_sort::Matrix{Float64}
Body::Matrix{Float64}
1 ─      (array_to_sort = Main.rand(2, 3))
│   %2 = Base._sortslices::Core.Const(Base._sortslices)
│   %3 = array_to_sort::Matrix{Float64}
│   %4 = Core.apply_type(Main.Val, 1)::Core.Const(Val{1})
│   %5 = (%4)()::Core.Const(Val{1}())
│   %6 = (%2)(%3, %5)::Matrix{Float64}
│        (sorted = %6)
└──      return %6
```
Benchmarking shows a 20% performance improvement
```jl
julia> @btime main();
  595.972 ns (19 allocations: 896 bytes)

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1389968985","pull_request_review_id":1725718694,"id":1389968985,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5S2T5Z","diff_hunk":"@@ -1880,6 +1880,196 @@ function adce_pass!(ir::IRCode, inlining::Union{Nothing,InliningState}=nothing)
     return complete(compact)
 end
 
+function arrayptr(P::Union{Type{Ptr{T}},Type{Ptr{Cvoid}}}, x::Array{T}, i::Int)::P where T
+    # Based on unsafe_convert for RefArray
+    if allocatedinline(T)
+        p = pointer(x, i)
+    elseif isconcretetype(T) && ismutabletype(T)
+        p = pointer_from_objref(x[i])
+    else
+        p = pointerref(Ptr{Ptr{Cvoid}}(pointer(x, i)), 1, Core.sizeof(Ptr{Cvoid}))
+    end
+    return p
+end
+
+struct CongruenceClass
+    ssa::Int
+    blockidx::Int
+end
+
+function perform_symbolic_evaluation(stmt::Expr, ssa_to_ssa)
+    # rename all SSAValues
+    # taken from renumber_ir_elements!
+    if stmt.head !== :enter && !is_meta_expr_head(stmt.head)
+
+        # key = similar(stmt.args, length(stmt.args)+1)
+        # copyto!(key, stmt.args)
+        # key[end] = stmt.head
+        # for (i, arg) in enumerate(stmt.args)
+        #     if isa(arg, SSAValue)
+        #         key[i] = SSAValue(ssa_to_ssa[arg.id])
+        #     end
+        # end
+        # svec(key...)
+
+        key = ccall(:jl_alloc_svec, SimpleVector, (Csize_t,), length(stmt.args)+1);
+        ptr = convert(Ptr{Ptr{Cvoid}}, pointer_from_objref(key)) + sizeof(Ptr{Cvoid})
+        for (i, arg) in enumerate(stmt.args)
+            if isa(arg, SSAValue)
+                unsafe_store!(ptr, ccall(:jl_box_ssavalue, Ptr{Any}, (Csize_t,), ssa_to_ssa[arg.id]), i)
+            else
+                unsafe_store!(ptr, arrayptr(Ptr{Nothing}, stmt.args, i), i)
+            end
+        end
+        unsafe_store!(ptr, pointer_from_objref(stmt.head), length(stmt.args)+1)
+        return key
+    else
+        return nothing
+    end
+end
+function perform_symbolic_evaluation(stmt::PhiNode, ssa_to_ssa, blockidx)
+    length(stmt.values) == 0 && return nothing
+

+    key = Vector{Any}(undef, no_of_edges*2 + 2)
+    copyto!(key, stmt.edges)
+    key[no_of_edges+1] = :phi
+    key[no_of_edges+2] = blockidx
+    copyto!(key, no_of_edges+3, stmt.values)
+
+    firstval = nothing
+    allthesame = true
+    deletions = 0
+    for j in eachindex(stmt.values)

enables common subexpression elimination, fixes #50887. This is based on the NewGVN pass in LLVM.

A possible avenue for increasing the number of eliminations would be to implement code hoisting (eg. an expression that is also present in all predecessors to a block could be moved to the immediate dominator and then replaced; alternatively we could replace it with a phi node) or Partial Redundancy Elimination.

I'm not sure when it would be optimal for this pass to run, the effects of a function may allow a non inlined call to be eliminated completely whilst when inlined only parts may be deleted.

For small functions most of the time is spent on allocating two arrays (`ssa_to_ssa` and the array backing `val_to_ssa`). For larger functions the `get!` dominates, #51091 speeds up `get!` 2x. I don't think there any easy optimizations left, one possible avenue would be computing use lists (a list of every instruction in which a `SSAValue` is used) and using that to not have to iterate over This is based on the NewGVN pass in LLVM.

A possible avenue for increasing the number of eliminations would be to implement code hoisting (eg. an expression that is also present in all predecessors to a block could be moved to the immediate dominator and then replaced; alternatively we could replace it with a phi node) or Partial Redundancy Elimination.

I'm not sure when it would be optimal for this pass to run, the effects of a function may allow a non inlined call to be eliminated completely whilst when inlined only parts may be deleted.

For small functions most of the time is spent on allocating two arrays (`ssa_to_ssa` and the array backing `val_to_ssa`). For larger functions the `get!` dominates, #51091 speeds up `get!` 2x. I don't think there any easy optimizations left, one possible avenue would be computing use lists (a list of every instruction in which a `SSAValue` is used) and using that to not have to iterate over every
